title: The Agricultural Revolution Creates a Rodent Problem
summary: When humans began farming in the Fertile Crescent around 10,000 BC, they created something that had never existed before: large, concentrated stores of grain. This was the foundation of civilisation — food that could be stockpiled against hunger, traded, and taxed. It was also an open invitation. Rats and mice, following the food as they always had, flooded into human settlements in numbers never previously possible. Rodents didn't just eat the grain — they contaminated it, destroyed it, and carried diseases through the densely packed human communities that farming had created. The agricultural revolution had accidentally created the optimal conditions for a partnership that would last ten thousand years.

title: Rats Move In
summary: Grain stores attracted rodents on a scale that threatened the viability of early farming settlements. A single pair of rats can produce hundreds of descendants in a year; a grain store large enough to feed a village was large enough to sustain a rat colony of extraordinary size. The rodents didn't merely compete with humans for food — they contaminated stores with urine and droppings, destroyed containers, and gnawed through the infrastructure of early settlements. Protecting stored food was not a minor inconvenience but an existential challenge for early agricultural communities. The solution was already lurking at the edge of the settlement, watching.

title: Cats Choose Us
summary: The oldest known evidence of cats living alongside humans is a burial at Shillourokambos in Cyprus, dated to around 7,500 BC: a wildcat (Felis silvestris lybica) interred beside a human, both oriented in the same direction, suggesting the cat mattered to whoever arranged the grave. Cyprus had no native wildcats — someone had brought one across the sea deliberately. Cats were not domesticated the way dogs, cattle, or pigs were: no human captured wildcats, selectively bred them for docility, and gradually produced a domestic animal. Instead, wildcats simply edged toward human settlements, drawn by the rodents that human food stores attracted. The humans who tolerated them got rodent control; the cats got easy prey. The relationship was mutually beneficial from the start — and it was the cats who initiated it.

title: The Most Important Job in the Ancient World
summary: As agriculture spread from the Fertile Crescent westward into Europe and eastward into Asia through the 7th millennium BC, cats followed — tracking the rodents that tracked the grain. A cat in a grain store was not a pet but infrastructure: it protected food supplies that entire communities depended on for survival. Evidence from Çatalhöyük in Turkey (around 7,000 BC) shows cats associated with human settlements long before any genetic changes suggesting deliberate breeding. The cat's value was purely functional — an autonomous, self-maintaining rodent control system that required no training, no feeding from human stores, and no supervision. No other animal in human history has been so useful while remaining so entirely on its own terms.

title: Egypt Makes Cats Gods
summary: Ancient Egypt's relationship with cats evolved over millennia. The goddess Bastet appeared as early as the Old Kingdom (around 2,700 BC) but was initially depicted as lioness-headed, associated with the protective ferocity of the sun. It was only during the Middle Kingdom and especially the Late Period (from around 1,000 BC) that Bastet became specifically cat-headed and associated with domestic cats, fertility, and the home. By the Late Period, the cat cult had reached extraordinary intensity: killing a cat was punishable by death; cats who died naturally were mummified and given elaborate burials; the city of Bubastis housed a great temple to Bastet where hundreds of thousands of cat mummies have been found. The Greek historian Herodotus recorded that when a house fire broke out, Egyptians would form a line to prevent the cat from running into the flames rather than fighting the fire. Egypt had elevated a useful pest controller into a sacred being — and in doing so gave the cat a cultural significance that would outlast the civilisation that created it.

title: The Cat as Symbol of Mystery
summary: Egypt's Late Period reverence for cats spread the animal's cultural significance far beyond its practical utility, particularly as trade and conquest distributed Egyptian ideas across the Mediterranean from around 800 BC onward. The cat became associated across the ancient world with grace, independence, mystery, and a quality of watching that felt almost supernatural — the unblinking gaze of an animal that seemed to see things humans couldn't. The very qualities that made cats useful pest controllers (stealth, patience, nocturnal activity, eyes that reflected light in the dark) gave them an aura of otherworldliness that humans interpreted as sacred in one era and demonic in another. The cat was the same animal in both cases. What changed was the theology.

title: Cats Hitchhike Across the Ancient World
summary: Phoenician traders, Greek merchants, and Roman legionaries carried cats wherever they went — not as passengers but as working crew, protecting grain and supplies from rodents on ships and in military camps. Genetic analysis of ancient cat remains shows a remarkable pattern: cats spread from the Near East in two waves, first with early farmers and then with ancient maritime traders. By the time of the Roman Empire, cats had reached Britain, Gaul, and Germany. They travelled not because anyone planned their dispersal but because wherever humans stored food, rodents followed, and wherever rodents went, cats were useful. The cat's global distribution was accomplished entirely through the cat's own utility.

title: Rome Brings Cats to Europe
summary: Roman expansion carried cats across Europe as a practical necessity — every military grain store and supply depot needed rodent control. Archaeological evidence shows cats appearing in Roman Britain, Roman Gaul, and Roman Germany from the first century AD. They were not luxury imports but working animals, recorded in administrative documents alongside other military supplies. As the Roman Empire expanded, contracted, and eventually collapsed, the cats remained — distributed across a continent they had reached entirely by making themselves useful. By the early medieval period, domestic cats were present throughout Europe. This was, unfortunately, a problem that a future pope would attempt to solve.

title: The Church Declares War on Cats
summary: Pope Gregory IX's papal bull Vox in Rama (1233) described a heretical sect whose initiation rites allegedly involved a black cat — and the document's vivid descriptions of demonic cats provided theological legitimation for folk suspicions already circulating in medieval Europe. Whether the bull directly caused systematic persecution or formalised pre-existing practices is debated by historians; what is clear is that cats, already associated with paganism through their Egyptian sacred status and with female independence through the women who kept them, became increasingly targeted across the following centuries. Black cats were particularly suspect; women who kept cats were more likely to be accused of witchcraft. Cat massacres became a feature of certain festivals. The cat population in parts of Europe declined dramatically — with consequences that nobody at the time connected to what followed.

title: The Rats Take Over
summary: With cat populations suppressed across much of Europe, rat populations expanded into the ecological space vacated. The black rat (Rattus rattus), which had spread across Europe along trade routes from Asia, thrived in the dense, grain-rich, poorly-sanitised conditions of medieval European towns and cities. Rats lived in walls, under floorboards, in grain stores and market stalls — in intimate proximity with the human population. They carried fleas; the fleas carried Yersinia pestis. The precise role of cat persecution in amplifying the Black Death is debated by historians — the disease's spread was driven by many factors including trade routes, human immune vulnerabilities, and climate. But fewer cats meant more rats, and more rats meant more vectors for the disease that was about to arrive from the east.

title: The Black Death and the Missing Cats
summary: The Black Death killed between 30 and 60% of Europe's population between 1347 and 1351 — the greatest demographic catastrophe in recorded history. Whether the persecution of cats meaningfully amplified its spread remains genuinely debated: the disease was catastrophically lethal regardless of rat density, and other factors (trade routes, climate, human immunity) were clearly primary drivers. What is clear is that cat persecution had removed a significant check on the rat populations that carried plague-infected fleas, and that the timing — two centuries of cat killing followed by the worst rat-borne epidemic in history — is at minimum a striking correlation. Medieval Europeans, processing the catastrophe around them, did not make this connection. They sometimes blamed the cats.

title: Cats Rehabilitated — Slowly
summary: After the Black Death, the practical case for cats reasserted itself. Populations decimated by plague needed every advantage in protecting surviving food stores from rodents. Cats reappeared in European art and literature with somewhat less demonic connotation — though the association with witchcraft persisted well into the 17th century and cat burning remained a feature of some festivals into the early modern period. The rehabilitation was incomplete and uneven. But the cat's fundamental utility — the thing that had made humans tolerate them in the first place — gradually overcame the theological objection. By the time the Age of Sail began, cats were once again respectable enough to be official members of ships' crews.

title: Every Ship Has a Cat
summary: The Age of Sail distributed cats across the globe more comprehensively than any deliberate colonisation effort could have managed. Every European vessel — merchant, naval, and exploration — carried cats as essential crew. Rats were an existential threat to ships: they gnawed through wooden hulls, destroyed rigging, contaminated water supplies, and ate through the food stores that kept crews alive. Ship's logs from the 15th century onward routinely record cats as official crew members, sometimes with individual names. The ship's cat was protected, fed, and mourned when lost. By the time European ships had reached every inhabited coast on Earth, they had distributed domestic cats — descendants of Felis silvestris lybica from the Near East — to every continent simultaneously.

title: Cats Conquer the New World
summary: European ships carried domestic cats to the Americas throughout the Age of Exploration. The Americas had native wild felids — jaguars, pumas, ocelots — but no domestic cats: the species had evolved in the Old World and been absent from the Western Hemisphere for millions of years. Domestic cats arrived with the first European settlements and spread rapidly. They found a continent whose wildlife had never encountered them and whose small mammals and birds had no evolved defence against a predator with their specific hunting techniques. Cats were simultaneously welcomed as pest controllers in settlements and devastating as uncontrolled predators in the wider ecosystem — a contradiction that has defined their relationship with conservation ever since.

title: Cats Reach Australia — and Stay
summary: The First Fleet brought domestic cats to Australia in 1788. Australia's native fauna had evolved in complete isolation from placental mammalian predators — its small mammals, ground-nesting birds, and reptiles had no instinctive defence against cats. Domestic cats that went feral found a continent of easy prey and no competition. Today, Australia has an estimated 6–8 million feral cats covering 99% of the continent's land area. They are implicated in the extinction of at least 25–34 native mammal species and kill an estimated 2 billion animals annually. The animal that Egypt had elevated to divine status became, in Australia, an ecological catastrophe — which is not the cat's fault, but is an accurate description of what happened.

title: The Most Successful Invasive Predator on Earth
summary: Domestic and feral cats are now recognised as among the most destructive invasive species on Earth. They have contributed to the extinction of at least 63 bird, mammal, and reptile species worldwide — more than any other introduced predator. In the United States alone, cats kill an estimated 1.3 to 4 billion birds and 6 to 22 billion mammals annually. On islands, where native species have no experience of mammalian predators, cat predation has been catastrophic. The animal that humans brought on ships to solve a rodent problem has become, in ecosystems where it doesn't belong, a rodent-scale problem itself. The cat's extraordinary effectiveness as a predator — the very quality that made it valuable — makes it lethal in environments it was never meant to reach.

title: 600 Million Cats
summary: The post-war era created conditions perfectly suited to cat population expansion. Commercial dry cat food — launched by Purina with Cat Chow in 1962 and rapidly adopted across the industry — removed the need for cats to hunt to survive. Post-war urbanisation concentrated humans — and cats — in dense environments where a small territory could sustain a well-fed animal indefinitely. Veterinary medicine extended cat lifespans; spaying and neutering of pet cats was promoted but not universally adopted. The global domestic cat population reached an estimated 600 million by the early 21st century, making it one of the most numerous large carnivores on Earth. This is the direct descendant of the Near Eastern wildcat that walked into a grain store ten thousand years ago — multiplied six hundred million times by the simple fact of being useful.

title: Edison Films Cats First
summary: In 1894, Thomas Edison's Black Maria studio in New Jersey filmed two cats wearing tiny boxing gloves, standing in a miniature boxing ring, and batting at each other while a man held them upright. 'The Boxing Cats' is one of the earliest filmed entertainments ever made — and it is a cat video. The internet did not invent cat content. The desire to film, share, and enjoy cats performing for human audiences appears to be as old as the technology that makes it possible. When Edison was developing cinema, one of the first applications was: what if we filmed cats? Humanity's relationship with cats is apparently deep enough that the invention of moving pictures produced cat videos within the same year.

title: The Internet Belongs to Cats
summary: 'I Can Has Cheezburger?' launched in January 2007 and within a year had become one of the most visited websites on the internet. What followed was not a trend but a civilisational condition: cats became the dominant content category of the digital age. YouTube's most-watched non-music content is overwhelmingly cat videos. Grumpy Cat generated substantial commercial revenue — some estimates reaching $100 million, though the figure is disputed — and became arguably the most recognisable animal face on Earth after Mickey Mouse. Nyan Cat sold as an NFT for 300 ETH ($590,000 at the time). The qualities that made cats suspicious to medieval Christians — the inscrutability, the independence, the sense that they are watching something humans cannot see — make them compelling to internet audiences for exactly the same reasons. The cat's aloofness reads, on a screen, as personality.

title: Worshipped Again
summary: By the 2010s, cats had achieved something that would have been recognisable to an ancient Egyptian priest and baffling to a medieval inquisitor: they were worshipped again. Not in temples, but on screens — billions of screens, simultaneously, across every country on Earth. The cat that walked into a human settlement ten thousand years ago because there were rodents to catch had, by a chain of events involving agriculture, theology, plague, sailing ships, and fibre optic cable, become the most represented animal in human visual culture. It did not seek this outcome. It did not need to. The cat has always been very good at finding the place where it is most comfortable and staying there until the humans arrange themselves around it.

title: The Cat Becomes a Companion
summary: Somewhere in the early modern period — gradually, unevenly, without a single moment of decision — the cat's relationship with humans changed. It had been a working animal: tolerated for its utility, not kept for companionship. By the 17th century, cats were appearing in aristocratic portraits sitting in laps, in domestic literature as named individuals with personalities, and in the writings of Montaigne, who in 1580 asked the question that cat owners have been asking ever since: 'When I play with my cat, how do I know she is not playing with me rather than I with her?' This was a new question. It assumed a subject on the other side of the relationship — an animal with its own interior life and its own form of agency. The cat was ceasing to be infrastructure and becoming a presence. The transition was complete enough by the 18th century that Samuel Johnson famously walked to the market himself to buy oysters for his cat Hodge rather than send a servant, not wanting Hodge to be disliked. This is not how you think about a rodent control system.

title: The Crystal Palace Invents the Pedigree Cat
summary: On July 13, 1871, artist and cat enthusiast Harrison Weir organised the first cat show at Crystal Palace in London. Approximately 160 cats were exhibited; thousands of Victorians attended. Weir had invented something: breed standards for cats. He wrote the rules that defined what a Persian should look like, what a Siamese should measure, what constituted a correct tabby pattern. The show created an entirely new cultural category — the pedigree cat, the show cat, the cat as object of aesthetic appreciation and competitive display rather than functional rodent control. The National Cat Club was founded in 1887; the Governing Council of the Cat Fancy in 1910; cat registries proliferated across Europe and America. By the early 20th century, keeping a specific breed of cat had become a statement of taste, class, and sensibility. The cat had travelled from wildcat to working animal to companion to aesthetic object in ten thousand years — and the final step took about thirty.

title: Schrödinger's Cat Enters the Language
summary: In a 1935 letter to Einstein, the physicist Erwin Schrödinger proposed a thought experiment to illustrate the absurdity of applying quantum superposition to macroscopic objects: a cat sealed in a box with a radioactive atom and a vial of poison. Until the box is opened and the atom's state observed, quantum mechanics appears to hold that the cat is simultaneously alive and dead. Schrödinger intended this as a reductio ad absurdum — evidence that quantum mechanics was incomplete. Instead, it became the most famous thought experiment in physics, and the cat became the symbol of quantum indeterminacy, observer effects, and the deep strangeness of reality at the subatomic level. The cat was chosen because it was a common, legible animal whose fate anyone could understand — the thought experiment works because you care whether the cat lives or dies. By the late 20th century, 'Schrödinger's cat' had entered ordinary language as a shorthand for any situation of unresolved superposition. The animal that had been a symbol of mystery since Egypt was now the symbol of the deepest mystery in modern physics.

title: The Parasite That Cats Gave the World
summary: Toxoplasma gondii is a parasite discovered in 1908 that can only complete its sexual reproductive cycle in the intestines of cats. Infected cats shed oocysts in their faeces; these survive in soil for months and infect any warm-blooded animal that encounters them. The parasite is estimated to infect approximately one-third of the global human population — making it one of the most widespread parasitic infections on Earth. In most people with healthy immune systems, infection produces no symptoms. In immunocompromised individuals and foetuses, it causes serious disease. More remarkably, some research suggests that chronic infection may measurably influence human behaviour — correlating with increased risk-taking, slower reaction times, and statistically elevated rates of certain mental health conditions, though causation remains debated. The parasite manipulates rodent behaviour with extraordinary precision — infected mice lose their fear of cat urine and are drawn toward it, making them easier prey and completing the parasite's lifecycle. Whether cats have been quietly influencing human behaviour for ten thousand years via a neurological parasite is one of the stranger questions in biology. The answer appears to be: possibly.

title: The Conservation War Over Cats
summary: As ecological science developed through the 20th century, the scale of cat predation on wildlife became increasingly documented and increasingly alarming. Conservation biologists and cat welfare advocates found themselves in direct and often bitter conflict over what to do. Trap-Neuter-Return (TNR) programmes — in which feral cats are sterilised and returned to their territory — became the dominant humane management strategy in many countries, championed by animal welfare organisations. Conservation biologists responded that TNR does not meaningfully reduce feral cat populations or their predation impact, and that returning apex predators to ecosystems already stripped of native species is ecologically indefensible. On islands — where native species have no evolved defence against cats — eradication programmes have been the only ecologically effective solution, and several have succeeded dramatically. The conflict between the people who love cats and the people whose job is protecting everything cats kill has become one of the most intractable disputes in conservation — a direct consequence of ten thousand years of bringing cats everywhere humans went.
